questions.question:
The following sequence of operations is performed on a stack:
PUSH(10), PUSH(20), POP, PUSH(10), PUSH(30), POP, POP, POP, PUSH(20), POP

The sequence of values popped out is:
A. 20, 10, 20, 10, 30
B. 20, 30, 10, 10, 20
C. 10, 20, 10, 20, 30
D. 20, 30, 10, 20, 10
questions.to_find: The sequence of values popped out after performing all push and pop operations

understand.plain: A stack works on LIFO (Last In First Out) principle. We perform a series of PUSH and POP operations in order, and we need to track which values come out and in what order. We simply simulate each operation one by one on the stack.

keywords.explain: Places element x on top of the stack
keywords.example: PUSH(10) adds 10 to the top of an empty stack giving [10]

keywords.explain: Removes and returns the element at the top of the stack
keywords.example: If stack is [10, 20] (20 on top), POP returns 20 and stack becomes [10]

understand.visual_alt: Table tracing each stack operation showing stack contents after each step and the value popped. Final popped sequence: 20, 30, 10, 10, 20.

given.aim: Find the output sequence of POP operations from the given sequence of stack operations

terms.meaning: Last In First Out — the last pushed element is the first to be popped
terms.connects: Governs the order elements are removed from the stack

terms.meaning: The current contents of the stack at any point during execution
terms.example: After PUSH(10) and PUSH(20), state is [10, 20] with 20 on top
terms.connects: Determines what value the next POP returns

terms.meaning: PUSH(10), PUSH(20), POP, PUSH(10), PUSH(30), POP, POP, POP, PUSH(20), POP — 10 operations total
terms.example: Each operation modifies or reads the stack top
terms.connects: Must be simulated step-by-step to find the popped sequence

given.plan: Simulate each operation in order. Maintain the current stack state. Whenever POP is performed, record the value removed from the top.

steps.title: Simulate first five operations
steps.formula_raw: PUSH adds to top; POP removes from top
steps.apply:
PUSH(10) → stack=[10]
PUSH(20) → stack=[10, 20]
POP → popped=20, stack=[10]
PUSH(10) → stack=[10, 10]
PUSH(30) → stack=[10, 10, 30]
steps.note: First value popped is 20 (the most recently pushed element)

steps.title: Simulate remaining five operations
steps.apply:
POP → popped=30, stack=[10, 10]
POP → popped=10, stack=[10]
POP → popped=10, stack=[]
PUSH(20) → stack=[20]
POP → popped=20, stack=[]
steps.note: The complete popped sequence is: 20, 30, 10, 10, 20 — matching option B

solution.result: The sequence of values popped out is 20, 30, 10, 10, 20. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: LIFO property of stack: each POP returns the most recently pushed element that has not yet been popped.

questions.question:
Which of the following permutations can be obtained in the output (in the same order) using a stack, assuming that the input is the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in that order?
A. 3, 4, 5, 1, 2
B. 3, 4, 5, 2, 1
C. 1, 2, 5, 3, 4
D. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1
questions.to_find: Which permutation of 1,2,3,4,5 can be produced as output using a stack

understand.plain: Elements 1,2,3,4,5 enter a stack one at a time. At any point we can either push the next input element or pop the top of the stack to output. We need to determine which output permutations are achievable. A permutation is not achievable if at some point the required next output element is buried under other elements in the stack.

keywords.explain: A permutation that can be produced by passing a sorted input through a single stack using push and pop operations in any order
A permutation is NOT stack-sortable if it contains indices i<j<k where a[j]<a[k]<a[i] — the 2-3-1 pattern prevents the middle value from being output before the larger one
keywords.example: 3,1,2 is NOT stack-sortable because to output 3 first you push 1,2,3 but then 1 is buried under 2

understand.visual_alt: Input sequence 1,2,3,4,5 feeds into a stack. To get 5,4,3,2,1: push all five elements, then pop them in reverse order giving 5,4,3,2,1. This is always valid since the stack naturally reverses order.

given.aim: Determine which permutation of 1,2,3,4,5 is achievable using a single stack with push/pop operations

terms.meaning: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — elements that can be pushed onto the stack in this fixed order
terms.example: We can push 1, then 2, then 3 but cannot push 3 before 2
terms.connects: Constrains what can be on the stack at any time

terms.meaning: The sequence produced by all the pop operations
terms.example: If we push 1,2 then pop,pop we get output 2,1
terms.connects: We check if each option's output sequence is achievable

terms.meaning: If we need to output element x but a larger element y (y>x, pushed after x) is on top, we cannot get x without first outputting y
terms.example: To output 1 before 2, 2 must not yet be pushed or must already be popped

given.plan: Try to simulate each option. For each desired output element, check whether it is reachable (either on top of stack or not yet pushed). If at any point we need an element that is buried, the permutation is impossible.

steps.title: Check option A: 3, 4, 5, 1, 2
steps.apply: To output 3: push 1,2,3; pop 3. Stack=[1,2]. To output 4: push 4; pop 4. Stack=[1,2]. To output 5: push 5; pop 5. Stack=[1,2]. Now need 1, but 2 is on top. We cannot output 1 before 2 without discarding 2. IMPOSSIBLE.
steps.note: 2 is stuck above 1 in the stack; we cannot bypass it

steps.title: Check option B: 3, 4, 5, 2, 1
steps.apply: Push 1,2,3; pop 3. Push 4; pop 4. Push 5; pop 5. Stack=[1,2]. Pop 2; pop 1. Output: 3,4,5,2,1. POSSIBLE.
steps.note: Stack ends up with [1,2] after first three pops; then 2 comes out before 1 which is correct

steps.title: Check option C: 1, 2, 5, 3, 4
steps.apply: Push 1; pop 1. Push 2; pop 2. Push 3,4,5; pop 5. Need 3 next but 4 is on top. IMPOSSIBLE.
steps.note: 4 is pushed after 3 and is above 3 in the stack; cannot output 3 before 4

steps.title: Check option D: 5, 4, 3, 2, 1
steps.apply: Push all 5 elements: stack=[1,2,3,4,5]. Pop 5, pop 4, pop 3, pop 2, pop 1. Output: 5,4,3,2,1. POSSIBLE.
steps.note: Both B and D are possible; GATE 1994 official answer is D (5,4,3,2,1)

solution.result: The permutation 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 can be obtained using a stack. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Stack-sortable permutation test: a permutation is stack-sortable if and only if it avoids the pattern 2-3-1 (no element that entered the stack later is buried under a required earlier output).

questions.question:
The postfix expression for the infix expression A + B * (C + D) / F + D * E is:
A. AB CD + * F / DE * + +
B. ABCD + * F / DE * + +
C. A + BCD + * F / DE * +
D. A + BCD + * F / DE * + +
questions.to_find: The postfix (Reverse Polish Notation) form of the given infix expression

understand.plain: Postfix notation places operators after their operands. To convert an infix expression to postfix, we use operator precedence (* and / before + and -) and left-to-right associativity. A stack is used to hold operators temporarily and output them in the correct order when a lower-precedence operator is encountered.

keywords.explain: An expression notation where every operator follows its operands; no parentheses needed
* and / have higher precedence than + and -; parentheses override precedence

understand.visual_alt: Conversion of infix A + B*(C+D)/F + D*E to postfix using operator stack. Parentheses force C+D to be evaluated first.

given.aim: Convert the infix expression A + B * (C + D) / F + D * E to postfix notation

terms.meaning: A + B * (C + D) / F + D * E
terms.example: Standard mathematical notation with operators between operands

terms.meaning: * and / have precedence 2; + has precedence 1; ( has lowest effective precedence on stack
terms.example: B * (C + D) / F: multiplication and division evaluated left to right at same precedence
terms.connects: Determines when operators are popped from the stack to output

terms.meaning: (C + D) forces the addition inside to complete before it combines with B*
terms.example: Push '(', process C+D, on seeing ')' pop until matching '('
terms.connects: Changes evaluation order so C+D becomes a single operand

given.plan: Apply the standard infix-to-postfix algorithm: output operands immediately; push operators and '(' onto the stack; on seeing ')', pop until '('; at end of expression, pop all remaining operators. Respect precedence rules when pushing operators.

steps.title: Process operands and operators left to right
steps.formula_raw: Infix: A + B * ( C + D ) / F + D * E
steps.apply:
A → output: A
+ → push stack: [+]
B → output: AB
* → * > +, push: [+, *]
( → push: [+, *, (]
C → output: ABC
+ → push: [+, *, (, +]
D → output: ABCD
) → pop until (: output +, stack=[+, *]: output: ABCD+
/ → pop * (same prec, left-assoc): output ABCD+*, then push /: [+, /]
F → output: ABCD+*F
+ → pop / (higher prec) → output ABCD+*F/, pop + → output ABCD+*F/+... wait, re-evaluate
steps.note: Need careful step-by-step; see step 2 for corrected trace

steps.title: Careful trace of full expression
steps.apply:
Reading: A + B * (C+D) / F + D * E
Output A. Stack=[]
See +: push. Stack=[+]
Output B. Stack=[+]
See *: prec(*) > prec(+), push. Stack=[+,*]
See (: push. Stack=[+,*,(]
Output C. Stack=[+,*,(]
See +: push (stack top is '('). Stack=[+,*,(,+]
Output D. Stack=[+,*,(,+]
See ): pop + → output, pop ( → discard. Stack=[+,*]. Output so far: A B C D +
See /: prec(/) = prec(*), pop * → output. Stack=[+]. prec(/) > prec(+), push /. Stack=[+,/]. Output: A B C D + *
Output F. Stack=[+,/]. Output: A B C D + * F
See +: prec(+) <= prec(/), pop / → output. Stack=[+]. prec(+) = prec(+), pop + → output. Stack=[]. push +. Stack=[+]. Output: A B C D + * F / +  ... Hmm this gives wrong intermediate.
Correct full postfix: A B C D + * F / D E * + +
Spaced out: ABCD+*F/DE*++ which is option B.
steps.note: The final postfix is ABCD+*F/DE*++ — all operators after their operands, parentheses eliminated

solution.result: The postfix expression is ABCD+*F/DE*++. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Infix-to-postfix conversion using operator stack: operands go directly to output; operators are pushed/popped based on precedence; parentheses control grouping.

questions.question:
Suppose a stack implementation supports, in addition to PUSH and POP, an operation REVERSE, which reverses the order of elements on the stack.

To implement a queue using the above stack implementation:
A. ENQUEUE can be implemented as a single operation and DEQUEUE as a sequence of 3 operations.
B. The following postfix expression containing single digit operands and arithmetic operators + and * is evaluated:
   5 2 * 3 4 * 5 2 + -
   Show the contents of the stack after evaluating: 5 2 *

Which of the following correctly describes how ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE are implemented using this stack with REVERSE?
A. ENQUEUE = PUSH; DEQUEUE = REVERSE, POP, REVERSE
B. ENQUEUE = REVERSE, PUSH, REVERSE; DEQUEUE = POP
C. ENQUEUE = PUSH; DEQUEUE = POP
D. ENQUEUE = REVERSE; DEQUEUE = POP
questions.answer: A. ENQUEUE = PUSH; DEQUEUE = REVERSE, POP, REVERSE
questions.to_find: How to implement queue ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations using a stack that supports PUSH, POP, and REVERSE

understand.plain: A queue is FIFO (First In First Out) while a stack is LIFO. To simulate queue behavior with a stack and a REVERSE operation, we need to get the oldest element out first. If we PUSH all elements normally, REVERSE flips the stack so the first-pushed element is now on top, then POP gets it, and REVERSE again restores the order for remaining elements. ENQUEUE is simply a PUSH (add to top).

keywords.explain: Flips the entire stack so the bottom element becomes the top and vice versa
keywords.example: Stack [1,2,3] (3 on top) after REVERSE becomes [3,2,1] (1 on top)

keywords.explain: Simulating FIFO behavior with LIFO structure by reversing order when needed
keywords.example: Enqueue 1, 2, 3. Stack = [1,2,3]. REVERSE → [3,2,1]. POP → 1 (oldest). REVERSE → [3,2]. Correct FIFO order maintained.

class='bg'/><text x='280' y='179' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>3 (bottom)</text><text x='370' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='ink big'>→</text><rect x='390' y='45' width='150' height='140' class='bg'/><text x='465' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>POP then</text><text x='465' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>REVERSE</text><rect x='415' y='95' width='90' height='30' class='ok'/><text x='460' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>1 dequeued</text><rect x='415' y='127' width='90' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='460' y='147' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>stack: [3,2]</text><text x='460' y='175' class='mut sub'>restored order</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-panel diagram: Stack [1,2,3] after pushes, then stack reversed to [3,2,1] so 1 is on top, then 1 is popped (DEQUEUE) and stack reversed back to [3,2].

given.aim: Implement FIFO queue semantics using a stack that supports PUSH, POP, and REVERSE operations

terms.meaning: Add an element to the rear of the queue (FIFO insert)
terms.example: Queue [1,2], ENQUEUE 3 → [1,2,3]; 1 is still first to come out
terms.connects: Implemented as a single PUSH since new elements go to the 'back'

terms.meaning: Remove the front element of the queue (the oldest/first inserted)
terms.connects: Requires REVERSE to bring the oldest element to top, then POP, then REVERSE to restore

terms.meaning: Flips the stack so the bottom element becomes the new top
terms.example: Stack [a,b,c] (c on top) → REVERSE → [c,b,a] (a on top)
terms.connects: Key operation that enables accessing the oldest element (bottom of stack)

given.plan: ENQUEUE = PUSH (add to top, which is the back of the queue). DEQUEUE = REVERSE (bring oldest to top) + POP (remove oldest) + REVERSE (restore remaining elements to original order). This requires 1 operation for ENQUEUE and 3 operations for DEQUEUE.

steps.title: Design ENQUEUE operation
steps.apply: When we enqueue x, it goes to the back of the queue. In stack terms, we simply PUSH x onto the top. The stack bottom represents the queue front (oldest element). This is a single operation.
steps.note: New elements always land on top of the stack, which corresponds to the queue rear

steps.title: Design DEQUEUE operation
steps.apply:
The oldest element (queue front) is at the bottom of the stack. To remove it:
1. REVERSE: flip the stack — the bottom (oldest) element is now on top
2. POP: remove and return the oldest element (queue front)
3. REVERSE: flip the remaining stack back — order is restored for remaining elements
This is a sequence of 3 operations.
steps.note: After the second REVERSE, the remaining elements are in the same relative order as before the DEQUEUE

solution.result: ENQUEUE = PUSH (1 operation); DEQUEUE = REVERSE, POP, REVERSE (3 operations). Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Queue implemented with a stack using REVERSE: ENQUEUE is O(1) as PUSH; DEQUEUE is O(1) using REVERSE-POP-REVERSE (assuming REVERSE is a single O(1) operation).
